key_departures.impact: Oweh was a midseason trade acquisition who exploded for 7.5 sacks in 12 regular-season games with the Chargers, forming a menacing trio with Mack and Tuipulotu. He added 3.0 sacks and two forced fumbles in the Wild Card playoff loss. Signed a 4-year, $100M deal ($68M guaranteed) with Washington. A massive loss — he was arguably the Chargers' best pass rusher by season's end and leaves a significant gap in the rotation.

Johnson was the starting left guard for four seasons after being the Chargers' 2022 first-round pick. While a pass-blocking liability — leading all guards with 19 quick pressures in 2025 — he was a solid run blocker and the only reliable IOL through a catastrophic injury-riddled season. Signed 3yr/$49.5M ($32.4M gtd) with Cleveland. Combined with Bozeman's retirement and Becton's release, the Chargers now have zero returning interior starters from 2025.

Starting center for the past two seasons retired in February. Ranked 30th among centers in pass-block win rate — well below replacement level. His departure opened the center need the Chargers addressed by signing Biadasz, but it was a catalyst for the full interior line rebuild.

Released March 4, saving just under $10M in cap space. Becton's lone season in Los Angeles was a disappointment — injuries and inconsistent play plagued him throughout. The release left the Chargers without a single returning interior starter besides the now-retired Bozeman and the departing Johnson.

St-Juste was a revelation after signing on a cheap 1yr/$2.5M deal in 2025. He posted the 11th-best overall coverage grade among 114 qualified CBs per PFF, allowing a 60.9 passer rating when targeted, with 7 pass deflections and an INT. Signed a 2yr/$10M deal with the Packers. While not a starter, his departure removes the Chargers' best quality depth CB and creates a pressing need for secondary depth.

Rotational DT who started 20 of 41 games over four seasons with the Chargers. Suffered an elbow injury in Week 7, limiting him to only 9 games in 2025. Signed a 1yr/$3M deal with Dallas. His departure thins the IDL depth behind Teair Tart.

Veteran TE released in early offseason, saving ~$4M in cap. His role had diminished throughout 2025. Not a starter — the release was part of the TE room overhaul under new OC Mike McDaniel. Limited impact on draft needs given his non-starter role.

Gilman spent 2025 split between the Chargers and Ravens after the Oweh trade. He finished with a career-high 90 tackles. Signed a 3yr/$24.75M deal with the Chiefs. His departure is a secondary concern given the Chargers' depth at safety with Derwin James and others.

signings.analysis: Mack is back on a 1yr/$18M fully guaranteed deal — identical structure to 2025. He remains productive but missed four games with an elbow injury in 2025 and his counting stats declined. The 1-year structure signals this is a bridge, not a long-term solution at EDGE. With Oweh gone to Washington, the Chargers now have Mack (age 35, bridge) and Tuli Tuipulotu (13.5 sacks in 2025, extension-eligible) as their top two EDGEs. They still need a third controlled edge rusher. EDGE is partially addressed at best — Mack stabilizes the room but the long-term depth concern remains real.

Extended January 26 before the market opened — the earliest and clearest priority move by GM Joe Hortiz. Tart was the Chargers' most impactful IDL, playing all 17 games and recording 12 run stops (7th in NFL). Three-year deal with $20M guaranteed is a real investment. He anchors the interior but Ogbonnia's departure leaves the IDL thin behind him. They likely need one more rotational DT via draft or budget free agent — this is a partial fix, not a full solution.

Signed March 6 before the legal tampering window even opened, filling the urgent center need after Bozeman's retirement. Three-year deal at $10M AAV is mid-market for a center and represents a clear upgrade — Biadasz ranked 15th in pass-block win rate vs. Bozeman's 30th. He graded 7th among centers with at least 3,500 snaps since 2021 per PFF. However, the Chargers still have zero returning starters at guard (both Johnson gone and Becton cut), so while C is addressed, the overall IOL situation remains dire. IOL need is still critical at the guard spots — this signing only partially resolves the position group.

Signed March 10 with 2yr/$13M deal. Strange started 43 of 44 career games, including 14 at right guard for Miami in 2025 under OC Mike McDaniel — giving him immediate familiarity with the new scheme. He could compete for the starting RG spot vacated by Becton. However, his pass-blocking has been a documented concern, and analysis notes he would be a liability if starting for a team that needs to protect Herbert. This is a genuine addition to the guard room but not a true starter-quality solution. With the top free agent guards now off the board, Strange and Penning are the two best options the Chargers have at guard — the need is partially addressed but remains a priority for the draft.

Re-signed on a 1yr/$4.5M deal. The Chargers traded for Penning midseason from the Saints and started him at left tackle, where he struggled mightily — allowing a team-high 8 pressures and 1 sack in one game. Analysis suggests his best role is guard, not tackle. Provides experienced line depth and can compete for a guard spot in camp. One-year deal with minimal guarantees signals depth/competition body, not a defined starter. Minimal impact on the IOL need.

Signed March 9 on 3yr/$24.3M ($17M gtd) — makes him the NFL's highest-paid blocking tight end. After releasing Dissly and having only rookie Oronde Gadsden II on the roster, the Chargers desperately needed a TE. Kolar is a premier run-blocking TE (ranked 6th with a 71.5 run-block grade per PFF) who fits McDaniel's zone-run heavy scheme and will help pave the way for Omarion Hampton. He showed pass-catching upside with 10 catches/142 yards/2 TDs in 2025 despite being depth in Baltimore. Three-year deal with real money signals genuine investment. Gadsden II handles receiving duties; Kolar handles blocking. TE is fully addressed as a starting tandem need.

Signed March 8 on 2yr/$7.5M deal (co-highest paid FB in NFL per AAV). A direct reunion with new OC Mike McDaniel, who used Ingold in 37% of offensive snaps during their time together in Miami. Pro Bowl fullback in 2023. McDaniel's offense features the FB heavily in zone-run blocking and as a pass-catcher out of the backfield. This signing is about scheme fit and the run game infrastructure around Hampton, not addressing a stated positional need. Fills a specific system role that didn't previously exist on this roster.

Re-signed March 10 on 1yr/$8.52M deal. Allen recorded 81 catches/777 yards/4 TDs in 2025 and became the franchise's all-time reception leader. His rapport with Herbert is exceptional and he serves as the WR2 behind Ladd McConkey. However, the 1-year deal with only $2.255M guaranteed is a clear bridge/aging-vet structure — no long-term commitment. At 34, this keeps the WR room functional but does not solve the long-term WR depth question. The Chargers still have McConkey, Johnston (5th-year option decision pending), Tre Harris (Year 2), and KeAndre Lambert-Smith behind Allen. WR is partially addressed for 2026 but remains a long-term need given Allen's age.

Tendered as an exclusive rights free agent March 6 at the minimum. Vidal had a career year in 2025 with 643 rushing yards and three scores. His return pairs him with Omarion Hampton (2025 1st round pick) to form a solid RB tandem. ERFA tender is procedural retention — not a free market signing. No impact on positional need since Hampton is the featured back.
Extended as ERFA March 6. Patterson appeared in 6 games with 159 rushing yards and a touchdown. Pure depth addition — no impact on positional need.
Extended as ERFA March 6. A reliable special teams contributor over two seasons. Minimum-level retention — no impact on positional need.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Despite signing Biadasz (C, 3yr/$30M) and Strange (G, 2yr/$13M), IOL remains a Tier 1 need. The Chargers lost all three 2025 interior starters — Bozeman (retired), Johnson (Browns), Becton (cut). Biadasz addresses center. Strange is a shaky guard option with pass-protection concerns. One guard spot remains genuinely unresolved. They have no quality depth. The IOL need has been partially addressed at center but a starting-caliber left guard and overall depth remain critical draft/FA priorities. Still the team's biggest positional need.

Mack re-signed (1yr/$18M) stabilizes the room short-term, but Oweh departed on a 4yr/$100M deal to Washington — the Chargers' best pass rusher. Mack is 35 on a bridge deal; Tuipulotu (13.5 sacks) is the true top option but is extension-eligible and unresolved. The Chargers need a third, controlled edge rusher. EDGE need stays at Tier 2 — the 2026 starters are accounted for but the depth behind two aging/contract-year players is thin and the long-term picture is unclear.

St-Juste departed to Green Bay (2yr/$10M), removing the team's highest-graded depth CB. The Chargers retain Tarheeb Still and Cam Hart as their projected starters, but the depth behind them is now minimal. No CB signing was made in free agency. CB remains a Tier 2 need — the starters are serviceable but quality depth is gone, and this position should be targeted in the draft.

Kolar signing (3yr/$24.3M, $17M gtd) fully addresses the TE need. Kolar is the NFL's top-paid blocking TE and pairs with rookie Oronde Gadsden II (664 yards in 2025) to form a legitimate starting tandem. Will Dissly's departure and Tyler Conklin's exit are fully offset. TE drops from a pressing need to low/no priority — drafting a TE would be luxury.

Tart extension (3yr/$30M, $20M gtd) secures the starter, but Ogbonnia's departure to Dallas thins the rotation significantly. The Chargers have Tart and likely need at least one more rotational IDL. IDL remains a Tier 2 need — specifically for rotational depth behind Tart. A Day 2/3 draft pick or low-cost veteran signing is warranted.

Allen re-signs (1yr/$8.52M) but on a bridge deal with minimal guarantees. He is 34 and a clear WR2 option for one more season alongside McConkey. Long-term WR depth beyond McConkey is still unresolved — Johnston's 5th-year option is a pending decision, and Harris/Lambert-Smith are unproven. WR stays at Tier 3 — Allen patches the 2026 need but the medium-term outlook still warrants draft attention.

No LB activity in free agency — no signings, no notable departures of starters. Daiyan Henley is the projected starter after a disappointing 2025. All top LB free agents (Devin Lloyd, Bobby Okereke, Quay Walker, Nakobe Dean) found homes elsewhere. LB need unchanged — remains a mid-tier draft concern.

Herbert is locked in as the starter, but Trey Lance (backup) is still unsigned and there is active concern about having no experienced backup QB on the roster. Only DJ Uiagalelei (futures contract) is behind Herbert. The Chargers should address this — either re-signing Lance or finding a veteran backup — before it becomes a crisis. The QB backup need escalates slightly given the roster void.

key_additions.headline: Pro Bowl center brings immediate upgrade to Chargers' rebuilt interior OL, ranked 15th in pass-block win rate vs. Bozeman's 30th
NFL's highest-paid blocking TE fills glaring need, pairing with Gadsden II to form a run-game-first two-TE set under Mike McDaniel
Mack returns on another fully guaranteed 1-year deal to anchor the pass rush alongside Tuli Tuipulotu after Oweh's departure
Chargers lock up their best run-stopper early, preventing a second straight loss of a key IDL to free agency
McDaniel's former Dolphins guard joins LA to compete for starting RG job, bringing 43 career starts and scheme familiarity
Pro Bowl FB reunites with OC Mike McDaniel, bringing the zone-run blocking infrastructure critical to McDaniel's scheme
Franchise all-time receptions leader returns for another season as Herbert's security blanket alongside Ladd McConkey

key_losses.headline: Chargers' best pass rusher departs after posting 7.5 sacks in 12 games post-trade; Chargers declined to match 9-figure market
Four-year starting LG walks to Cleveland, leaving Chargers without a single returning 2025 interior starter
Cap casualty after injury-plagued, inconsistent lone season in LA completes the full interior offensive line teardown
Breakout CB (11th-best coverage grade in NFL per PFF) cashes in on elite season, leaving a hole in LA's secondary depth
Two-year starting center retires, triggering the Chargers' full interior OL overhaul heading into 2026
